steps.title: Pin down what the underlined claim needs
steps.content: The underlined sentence asserts that even in countries where almost everyone speaks Swahili, many of those speakers learned it as a second, third, or fourth language — not as a first language. Supporting evidence must show that the total Swahili-speaking population in such a country greatly exceeds the worldwide first-language total (about 15 million) cited in the passage.

steps.title: Match the data to that need
steps.content: Choice A reports that Tanzania has roughly 61 million Swahili speakers, which is far more than the ~15 million worldwide first-language total. Because Tanzania alone has many more Swahili speakers than there are first-language speakers globally, most Tanzanian speakers must have learned Swahili as a non-first language — exactly the claim.

steps.title: Eliminate the other choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Tanzania has approximately 61 million Swahili speakers...much more than...first language': directly demonstrates the gap the claim requires. ✓
  • Choice B — 'Tanzania is estimated to have at most 15 million Swahili speakers': misreads the table and fails to show that total speakers exceed first-language speakers. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Approximately 100 percent of the people who speak Swahili as their first language live in Kenya': an overreach the table does not support, and it does not address non-first-language acquisition. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Approximately 100 percent of Kenya's population speaks Swahili, while only about 25 percent of the Democratic Republic of the Congo's': compares two countries' speaker percentages but never contrasts total speakers with first-language speakers, so it cannot support the claim. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
